data.title: NioCorp Developments: Pre-Production, But Worth A Place In A Long-Term Portfolio
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I remember when rare earth minerals and mining companies were all the rage in the market news a few months ago as the US government began to invest in a few of them to lock up and secure supply lines. Well, now a lot of them have come down in price, and quite a lot too. NioCorp Developments (NB), for instance, peaked at just under $12 per share in October, now trading below $6 per share. Has anything fundamental changed between then and now for the company? The short answer is no. It's still pre-revenue and in the development stage for its Nebraska-based mining project focusing on Niobium, Scandium, and Titanium.

The company has all the qualities to be a long-term solid compounder. It's an investment in future demand for rare minerals and US dependency on other countries. For me, this is a solid Strong Buy right now in the market, especially after the pullback from the October highs.

Pre-Revenue - All Going to Plan So Far

As I mentioned in the first segment, NB is still pre-revenue but is progressing quite well. The company has managed to secure a lot of funding and contracts for the minerals being produced there, all before production has even started. The financing is a mix between equity and debt. Equity financing has contributed $380 million so far this year. In terms of share dilution, that comes out to a 65.7% increase, from 45.9 million to 76.1 million. Given that the stock price is also up over 300% this year, I think early investors might not care that much, though, to be frank. Investors who bought at the top in October might view it differently. But then again, the numbers were quite public at that point as to what the mine was going to produce over its 38-year expected lifetime.

Minerals - End Use and More Crucial Info

Processed Into

End Use

Market Growth

Supply Line

Niobium

Ferroniobium

Improve steel quality, pipelines, and solid-state lithium-ion batteries

Lithium-ion batteries - 23.22% CAGR until 2032.

Brazil controls 97% of the world's exploitable reserves.

Scandium

Scandium Oxide

Create ultra-strong aluminum alloys, aircraft frames, defense systems, and solid oxide fuel cells.

Aircraft frames - 5.8% CAGR until 2032. Oxide batteries - 31.2% CAGR until 2030.

China mostly, historically also Ukraine and Russia as a byproduct from uranium mining.

Titanium

Titanium Tetrachloride

White pigment, aerospace-grade alloys, medical implants.

Medical implants - 6.64% until 2030.

China and Australia hold over 70% of global reserves.

I've compiled above a table to show the main three minerals that the mine NB is working will be producing at first. There are a few others, but these are the first and most important ones. As the table shows, they are used in a lot of high-growth end markets. As with most of these mineral markets these days, the most crucial ones are end markets like batteries, EV usage, and semiconductors.

Worth knowing is that the US does import most of these minerals, if not all, because production is so small. Titanium, for instance, had only about 200 tonnes produced in the US, with reserves in the range of 2,000 tonnes. So not a large market. Scandium holds more importance as it's led to $10 million in funding from the Pentagon, as well as a partnership with Lockheed Martin (LMT). The US has for a long time been reliant on foreign producers of this, mostly China, Russia, and Ukraine, where it's a byproduct in other mining operations.

When NioCorp comes online to produce approximately 100 tonnes per year of scandium oxide – after being 100% dependent upon foreign producers

One site puts the price per kg of scandium at $3,197, meaning the expected production from Elk Creek would generate $31.97 million per year, based on that figure in the quote.

The other mineral would be Niobium, which puts NB in the unique position to be one of the larger domestic producers of this. But it's still not the largest. Iron Hill is shown to be the largest in the US, but there isn't an active project here, it seems. It's owned by Teck Resources (TECK), and there is a push by Canamera Energy Metals (EMETF) to start production here, but the mining claims are still unpatented and pending BLM registration.

Round Top, however, is being developed by USA Rare Earth (USAR) and TMRC in a joint partnership. It's still in pre-production, though. In Cave Peak there is mostly exploration, but no real push, it seems, to develop it at a faster pace and get it operational.

Dysprosium

Dysprosium Oxide - added to NdFeB magnets

Terbium

Terbium Oxide - Green phosphors

LED displays, lightning, lasers, fuel cells

LED Displays - 5.72% until 2030. Lasers - 7.06% until 2030.

Zero US production so far.

Above are the minerals that the company plans on expanding into as the mine progresses. For those that follow the mining industry, you might be familiar with a few of these commodities. For those who have read about the company MP Materials (MP) should definitely be aware of Neodymium. It's perhaps the biggest material being produced in their mine, Mountain Pass in California. What differentiates this company from NB is that it is in full production and is also now utilizing its own refinement plant. Historically, China has been the place where you ship your minerals to get processed and refined and then shipped back again. This is changing now, and companies like MP are trying to set up fully domestic supply chains instead. NB will still have to rely on China for refinement purposes, which means that the relationship between the US and China could impact its ability to handle the products. This is a serious risk consideration.

Elk Creek Long-Term Outlook

In a presentation from 2022, the company shows the current expected life span of the mine, the products mined there, and the reserves as well. In short, it has an expected life span of 38 years, mining 946,000 tonnes per year.

NB included an operating cost structure, which puts the total annual Opex at around $196 million per year, based on the 946,000 tonnes mined per year/throughput. In the company's earlier presentation, it did not include the REE in the revenues, as there was no feasibility study for this. Since the report was in 2022, and the prices have changed quite a bit, the old model suggests far higher revenues today than anticipated back then. For instance, the price of Ferroniobium was between $50 and $60 per kg in 2024, compared to the model in the presentation using $39.6.

In the company's more recent presentations, however, it does include a figure of $21.9 billion in gross revenues over the entire mine life, with a 68% average annual EBITDA margin as well ($14.892 billion EBITDA). All the permits are approved for the project, but the actual construction is yet to start. A conservative estimate would be about 3-4 years until the actual production could start, with perhaps 6 months for the ramp-up to finish. So we're looking at $403 million in EBITDA by 2032 at least. With an EV of $521 million right now, this puts the company at an FWD EV/EBITDA of 1.29. Considering the sector median is 9.17 on an FWD basis, this shows a serious upside potential in the medium term. At the 9x mark, it implies an EV of $3.6 billion, a 691% increase from today.

The issue that some might see here is that it will essentially increase to this figure and then stay flat. Meaning, are there any other expansions the company could make at the current location to increase the mine life or create new revenue streams? Being dependent on one location is a risk, of course, and there will be considerable volatility because revenues are also very tied to market prices.

Back in September of this year, the company performed several drilling tests at the site, which potentially could drastically increase the size of the reserves. Because these aren't yet confirmed and seem to be more about speculation, I'll focus on confirmed figures. There is still considerable value based on the confirmed figures from the company, so sticking with that seems better for the sake of this article.

The table above is trying to figure out the additional revenues that the rare earth minerals would add for the company.

(does not include rare earth production, metallurgical advancements, and mine plan improvements)

As the quote says from the last presentation, these REEs aren't accounted for in the feasibility study that NB has made. But it's clear that there would be considerable benefits from accounting for it. The revenue figures are again from the 2022 report. At the same EBITDA margin, the annual addition would be roughly $265.5 million per year, leading to nearly $700 million in annual EBITDA if the figures and estimates that NB has made are true. This increases the upside potential even further, but for me, crucially, also reduces the risk profile here by a wide margin. Even when not accounting for rare earth minerals, the project looks very attractive over the long term. It's, of course, a more risk-adjusted bet since there is no production yet. I'd assume that any halt or setback in getting production up will severely hit the share price. But because of the long-term positive revenue and profitability image here, I think the risk is worth it.

I've talked about the valuation a little already; even if it gets pushed out 1 or 2 years, the upside once operations are normalized and NB supposedly is generating $403 million, potentially $668 million in annual EBITDA, having an EV of just $521 million for me implies a serious undervaluation compared to the long-term value of the Elk Creek.

Risks

The main risk that revolves around the company right now is the project and construction being delayed further. Since no revenues have been generated yet and it relies on a mix of debt and equity financing, it would dilute, and frankly most likely will, shareholders a lot more before the first revenues are recorded. Because of the backing from the Pentagon, the Exim Bank as well, which is processing the $780 million debt financing application.

Because NB has already secured customer lines for scandium, for example, with LMT, that partially offsets some of the risks of having to sell on the spot market. Dilution and increased leverage are the main risks right now. With $162 million in cash, it can sustain at least 13 more quarters of operating expenses, based on the $12 million total reported for the last period.

The Full Picture

NB is a different play in the minerals industry, as it is a pure US-based company with serious growth prospects at its Elk Creek location. It has a large reserve of crucial materials that are used in a number of different end products, most of which are projected to show steady and consistent growth over the next few years.

If the company can manage to realize the figures from the mine feasibility study, showing $403 million in annual EBITDA and $265 million more if the rare earth minerals are accounted for, well then the company is trading for pennies on the dollar, so to speak. I think that's a fair statement considering the EV is below $550 million and we could be 4-5 years away from the mine being in full production. The company has a solid cash balance as well and support from the government too. It checks all the boxes for me as a speculative growth play. Rating NB shares a Strong Buy.

data.title: John Hancock 2010 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Positive investment conditions led to broad-based gains for the financial markets in the third quarter.
  • The fund, while positive in absolute terms, didn't keep pace with its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation was a modest detractor.

Market review and outlook

Financial assets delivered strong returns in the quarter, with both stocks and bonds registering gains. A favorable backdrop of positive global growth, falling interest rates across the developed markets, and a lack of pronounced negative news fueled a surge in investors' appetite for risk. Most notably, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by a quarter point in September, and the markets anticipated that additional easing could be in store over the months ahead. Investors were further cheered by positive corporate earnings reports and the relatively limited initial economic impact from tariffs.

These developments helped propel major equity indexes to a series of all-time highs. The United States led the way higher behind renewed enthusiasm about the artificial intelligence theme and the technology sector more broadly. Small-cap stocks, which benefited from the improving interest rate outlook and investors' preference for risk, performed particularly well. The emerging markets were another top performer, with China registering an impressive gain amid revived optimism about the country's economic outlook.

The global fixed-income markets also produced positive returns, reflecting the increasingly favorable shift in Fed policy. Longer-term debt and credit-oriented categories generally provided the best results.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation was a slight detractor from the fund's relative performance in the quarter. An underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks and corresponding overweights in defensive equities, mid caps, and the international markets were the primary drivers of the modest shortfall. An overweight in bonds, while consistent with the fund's mandate, also hurt results. On the other hand, diversification into a real assets portfolio—which was helped by its position in metals and mining stocks—contributed to performance. An overweight in emerging-market bonds in the fixed-income portfolio was a further positive.

Portfolio changes

Investment conditions remained broadly positive at the close of the period, highlighted by ongoing optimism related to economic conditions and global central bank policy. However, valuations have become fairly full across many areas of the equity and fixed-income markets. With this in mind, we maintain a diversified portfolio designed to hold up through a wide range of possible outcomes. We think it's better to keep a long-term view rather than trying to chase returns in specific categories. We believe this approach should hold the fund in good stead over time, and we think its merits may become increasingly apparent if the market backdrop becomes less favorable than investors are anticipating.

data.title: Invesco Limited Term California Municipal Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Fund performance

Invesco Limited Term California Municipal Fund Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) underperformed its style-specific index, the S&P Municipal California Investment Grade 4-7 Years Bond Index.

2 Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

3 Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In the third quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 2.87%, 1.58% and 2.66%, respectively. 1 The municipal market saw mixed performance: July and August continued the previous quarter's underperformance, but September brought strong performance in long duration municipals as US Treasuries rallied and municipal bond fund flows increased.
  • New municipal bond issuance reached $153 billion in the third quarter, bringing the year-to-date total to $437 billion. This is 13% higher than the same period in 2024 and significantly above previous years. 1
  • Municipal fund net flows were a highlight, totaling about $16.2 billion for the quarter and $31.8 billion year to date. 2
  • In September, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) made its first rate cut of the year, lowering the federal funds rate by 0.25%. The Fed cited increased downside risks to employment and reiterated its commitment to balancing its dual mandate of maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. Future policy decisions will depend on incoming data, the evolving outlook and the balance of risks. 3
  • We believe state and municipal budgets remain healthy. Although the pace of credit rating upgrades has slowed, credit fundamentals still appear strong and upgrades have outpaced downgrades so far this year. 4
  • The quarter ended with the threat of a government shutdown as Congress struggled to pass funding bills needed to keep federal agencies operating in the fiscal year beginning October 1st. 1
  • During periods of policy uncertainty, we rely on our experienced credit research team to identify and capitalize on marketplace dislocations in order to deliver value to shareholders.
  • Looking ahead, we see opportunities in the municipal bond market. With potential for further Fed rate cuts and steady new municipal issuance, we believe high absolute yields and strong fundamentals make municipals an appealing option.

Portfolio positioning

Yield Curve and Duration Positioning: Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook for duration and are looking to selectively add longer duration securities to the fund when appropriate and according to prospectus guidelines. We maintain an overweight in the shorter maturity segment of the yield curve given the fund's short duration mandate.

Sector Allocations: We maintain an overweight position in revenue bonds relative to state and local general obligation bonds. Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the airport, public power and higher education sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market as upgrades of credit ratings have continued to outpace downgrades. Various sectors have been under pressure due to uncertainty surrounding federal funding and tariff policy, as well as higher labor costs. We are monitoring these sectors closely and continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to find pockets of opportunity.

Performance highlights

The third quarter had a volatile start as record levels of new issuance and uncertainty related to fiscal and monetary policies weighed on municipal valuations. Momentum shifted in late August as labor market weakness fueled expectations that the Fed would resume monetary easing. Municipals rallied strongly through September and ended the quarter with positive returns. As expected, the Fed cut the federal funds rates by 0.25% in September and in its comments on future actions indicated a stance favoring easier monetary policy. Yields fell across the municipal yield curve, with yields on two- and five-year bonds down 0.35% and 0.41%, respectively. 5 Higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed for the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

The fund's exposure to bonds with effective durations between nine and 15 years added to relative return during the quarter. An underweight allocation to state general obligation bonds also contributed to relative performance.

Detractors from performance

Detractors from relative return this quarter included the following:

The fund's overweight in bonds with durations shorter than one year detracted from relative performance, as did security selection among bonds with durations between four and five years. Security selection in industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR) bonds and toll roads also detracted from relative performance.

data.title: Harbor Convertible Securities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The backdrop of continuing U.S. labor market weakness has been increasingly reflected in consumer sentiment surveys and upward trends in credit delinquencies. - BlueCove Limited

Market in Review

The risk rally continued during the third quarter, with U.S. equities gaining 8% and U.S. convertibles up nearly 9%. Though overall market volatility was muted, ongoing headlines in the Technology sector, which accounts for about 30% of the U.S. convertible bond market, provided a compelling opportunity set for the Harbor Convertible Securities Fund's ("Fund") security-selection emphasis.

Portfolio Performance

During the third quarter of 2025, the Harbor Convertible Securities Fund (Institutional Class) returned 9.07%, outperforming its benchmark, the ICE BofA US Convertible Index, which returned 8.58%.

Returns from security selection were positive, driven by names in the Technology sector. The performance of BlueCove's proprietary research insights were positive, led by sentiment signals. The strongest returns came in the medium (at-the-money) delta convertibles, but performance was also positive for low and high delta convertibles.

Retirement Class shares commenced operations on March 1, 2016. The performance attributed to the Retirement Class shares prior to that date is that of the Institutional Class shares. Performance prior to March 1, 2016 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of Retirement Class shares. During this period, Retirement Class shares would have had returns similar to, but somewhat higher than, Institutional Class shares due to the fact that Retirement Class shares represent interests in the same portfolio as Institutional Class shares but are subject to lower expenses.

Underweights and Overweights

The Fund seeks to be broadly neutral in duration, yield curve, and equity delta exposure versus the index. During the quarter, the largest sector positions were an overweight to Capital Goods and an underweight to Electrics and Consumer Cyclicals.

Since a large portion of the convertible bond universe is not rated, the Fund does not have a specific credit-quality bias. Risk continues to be focused on security selection in convertible bonds, with the Fund well-matched on delta exposures versus the benchmark. We seek to invest only in index-eligible securities and U.S. dollar securities, and we do not emphasize country selection versus the benchmark.

Outlook

The backdrop of continuing U.S. labor market weakness has been increasingly reflected in consumer sentiment surveys and upward trends in credit delinquencies. We believe these factors underpin concerns that expensive risky asset valuations belie macroeconomic fundamentals. BlueCove's U.S. Economic Regime Indicator shifted from "Contraction" to "Slowdown" in August, due to diminished risk of a trade war tipping the economy into recession. However, considering the labor market, our indicator remains close to the threshold that flags elevated recessionary risk. Were it to summon an increase in market volatility, we would expect this to be a positive backdrop for a scientific approach to security selection in the Fund, which seeks to capture this volatility through company-specific insights.

Risks

All investments involve risk including the possible loss of principal. There is a greater risk that the Funds will lose money because they invest in below- investment grade fixed income securities and unrated securities of similar credit quality (commonly referred to as "high-yield securities" or "junk bonds"). These securities are considered speculative because they have a higher risk of issuer default, are subject to greater price volatility and may be illiquid. The market for convertible securities is less liquid than the market for non-convertible corporate bonds. There are limitations inherent in every quantitative model. The value of securities selected using quantitative analysis can react differently to issuer, political, market, and economic developments than the market as a whole or securities selected using only fundamental analysis. Because the Funds may invest in securities of foreign issuers, an investment in the Funds is subject to special risks in addition to those of U.S. securities. These risks include heightened political and economic risks, greater volatility, currency fluctuations, higher transaction costs, delayed settlement, possible foreign controls on investment, possible sanctions by government bodies of other countries and less stringent investor protection and disclosure standards of foreign markets.

Benchmarks

The ICE BofA US Convertible Index is a market-capitalization weighted index that tracks the performance of publicly issued US dollar denominated convertible securities of US companies. Convertible securities where the underlying is a basket of equities qualify for inclusion in the index, as do mandatory and convertible preferred securities. Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Beta is a rolling three year, unless the Fund has a track record of less than three years, in which case it is a rolling one year.

Duration is a commonly used measure of the sensitivity of the price of a debt security, or the aggregate market value of a portfolio of debt securities, to change in interest rates. Securities with a longer duration are more sensitive to changes in interest rates and generally have more volatile prices than securities of comparable quality with a shorter duration.

Effective duration is the sensitivity of a bond's price against the benchmark yield curve.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Delta is the theoretical estimate of how much an option's value may change given a $1 move up or down in the underlying security. The Delta values range from -1 to +1, with 0 representing an option where the premium barely moves relative to price changes in the underlying stock.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Core Plus Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The third quarter of 2025 was marked by shifts in macroeconomic, geopolitical, fundamental, and supply and demand dynamics. Markets rebounded from the lows following the “Liberation Day” announcements as immediate economic impacts remained subdued. The final outcomes for tariffs and other policies remain unknown, as do their longer-term economic impacts. However, financial markets gained confidence over the quarter that the economy remained solid, inflation remained rangebound, and geopolitical risks showed early signs of easing.

Economic data in the U.S. continued to point to a resilient economy. Growth improved after a weak first half, with inflation staying contained and unemployment holding near historically low levels despite some downside surprises. This allowed the Federal Reserve (Fed) to resume easing policy with a 25-basis-point (BPS) reduction at the September Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting. The Fed has acknowledged that while elements of its mandate are in conflict, it has chosen to focus on protecting the labor market in the near term. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan. Geopolitical developments were mixed during the third quarter, with volatility re-emerging in the Russia/Ukraine conflict following the Alaska summit in August, while the Middle East saw some signs of progress.

The continuing market rebound resulted in generally positive financial market performance in the quarter. Fixed income sector performance was also positive, with interest rates lower during the period. Most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries, and the U.S. Treasury curve steepened slightly. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased by 11 bps, the 5-year Treasury yield decreased by 6 bps, the 10-year Treasury yield decreased by 8 bps, and the 30-year Treasury yield moved 4 bps lower.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund (Class I) returned 2.16% in the third quarter versus the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index return of 2.03%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund's underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact, as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and had positive excess returns.
  • Emerging Markets (EM) Sovereign: The Fund's overweight and selection in the EM sovereign sector was positive.
  • Investment Grade (IG) Corporates Selection: Issue selection versus the benchmark was positive for the period.

Performance Detractors

  • Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS): Allocation to RMBS over agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and selection within RMBS had a negative impact on performance.
  • Bank Loans Selection: Issue selection was negative.

Current Fund Strategy and Positioning

  • Increased exposure to MBS, EM corporate bonds, and EM local currency bonds.
  • Reduced RMBS, non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities, and cash.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund's sector allocation during the quarter, we continue to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

EM Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index returned 4.75% in 3Q, with EM high yield (HY) outperforming Em Ig. EM corporate debt returned 3.18%. In 3Q, the U.S. imposed a 50% tariff on Brazilian exports, straining ties and pushing Brazil toward China. Former president Jair Bolsonaro was convicted for attempting a coup after his 2022 loss. In Argentina, President Javier Milei's party suffered a major defeat to Peronists in Buenos Aires, sparking market volatility. Later, the U.S. pledged $20 billion in aid to support Milei's reforms. In Ecuador, President Noboa ended diesel subsidies, triggering widespread protests as fuel prices surged. The Fund's overall EM exposure was higher throughout the quarter. The Fund maintained a preference for U.S. dollar assets and Em Hy over Em Ig.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads ended 3Q in the low 70s—the lowest levels since 1998—while yields moved lower by 18 bps to 4.81%. Despite lower spreads and yields, IG saw positive fund flows in every week of 3Q. Institutional buyers remain active as well. Though supply for the quarter was heavier than the five-year average, it was easily absorbed. Upgrades outpaced downgrades year-to-date, though at a slower pace than in recent years. BBBs are at their lowest percentage of the overall market in a decade. We are focused on defensive industries given tight valuations, with a preference for utilities, pipelines, and capital goods.

HY Corporates: The HY market, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index, gained 2.54% in 3Q. Credit spreads tightened by 23 bps to +267 bps. Stronger-than-expected economic growth, a dovish Fed pivot that produced a 25-bps rate cut in September, resilient corporate earnings, and significant inflows contributed to performance. Media outperformed due to anticipated deregulation and positive merger and acquisition developments. Chemicals and packaging underperformed due to weak global demand.

Securitized: The securitized markets had positive total and excess returns for 3Q. Spreads tightened despite elevated issuance across all securitized sub-sectors as investors sought out additional income and spread within securitized sectors. We added agency MBS during the quarter as we felt the sector had lagged alternatives such as the IG corporate sector. While our strategy continues to focus on up-in-quality given signs of stress on the margin for certain borrowers, we're also selectively exploring individual transactions that present promising yield opportunities lower in the capital stack.

data.title: Gabelli SRI Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Social Responsible Investing

Socially responsible investing (SRI) refers to the proactive screening of companies that meet predetermined social guidelines. Specifically, the Fund excludes companies that derive 10% or more of their revenues from tobacco, cannabis, alcohol, gambling, or weapons production. This screen is relatively straightforward and transparent, incorporating a broad range of social criteria that have been utilized by asset owners for decades. The Fund otherwise relies on the Advisors' Private Market Value with a Catalyst™ approach to selecting underpriced securities. By incorporating values-based screens, the Fund may be able to minimize the risks associated with the identified industries, improving the overall return of the investment portfolio.

Investment Scorecard

The top contributor to the Fund in the third quarter was Resideo (REZI) (1.2% of net assets as of September 30, 2025; +96%), whose shares soared after the company reported better than expected second quarter results and announced it would pay $1.59 billion to permanently resolve the legacy environmental liabilities it assumed in conjunction with its spin-off from Honeywell (HON). With these liabilities resolved, the company was able to announce the planned separation of its ADI Global Distribution business and its Products and Solutions business into two separate public companies. Shares of global water solutions leader Xylem (XYL) (3.9%; +14%) rose, as the company reported solid Q2 results with revenue up 6% and earnings up 16%, driven by strength in its Measurement and Control Solutions and Water Solutions and Services businesses. Cavco (CVCO) (1.5%; +34%) shares climbed as it reported a 56% increase in fiscal first quarter EPS as demand for affordable manufactured homes remained healthy, and the company's insurance operations benefited from lower weather-related claims. The company also completed the acquisition of American Homestar Corp., which adds two manufacturing facilities and nineteen retail stores, and is expected to be accretive to earnings and cash flow. Finally, shares of Sony Group (SONY) (3.5%; +13%) gained on strong results in gaming and music; the company completed the spin-off of its Financial Services business just after quarter end.

Detractors included BellRing Brands (BRBR) (1.7%; -37%), whose shares again fell on investor concerns about increasing competition and slowing growth in the protein shake category; we continue to believe BellRing is a long-term winner and could be a takeover target for a larger food and beverage company. CNH Industrial (CNH) (2.4%; -16%) shares declined amid weakness in prices and demand for crops such as soybeans and corn that are likely to stall a recovery in demand for tractors and combines in 2026. Daimler Truck (DTRUY) (1.3%; -13%) reported a 61% decline in second quarter EPS as economic softness and tariff uncertainty negatively impacted demand for trucks, particularly in the U.S. Nevertheless, Daimler appears to be well positioned to weather current industry weakness and participate in the eventual recovery, given its strong financial position and market leading position in North America. Finally, Atlanta Braves Holdings (BATRA) (1.3%; -11%) shares declined during the quarter, giving back some of their strong returns over the last two years. Sports franchise values remain strong, and value is likely to eventually be surfaced in the stock through a sale of the team/company.

data.title: BlackRock Advantage Large Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 6.46% (Institutional shares) and 6.41% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the third quarter of 2025.
  • The fund's outperformance of its benchmark was largely driven by strong results from fundamental insights, with both traditional value and quality measures contributing meaningfully. Sentiment-driven signals also added to returns. Macro insights were more mixed, with some successfully capturing sector trends while others struggled amid shifting market themes.
  • From a sector-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. However, there were slight overweight allocations to the information technology (IT) and consumer discretionary sectors, and slight underweight exposures to the utilities and energy sectors.

Contributors

Fundamental insights were the most consistent drivers of performance. Value-focused insights, such as those evaluating companies with strong operating income relative to their stock price, contributed with an overweight exposure to the IT sector. Insights favoring firms investing heavily in research and development provided further gains. Within quality, signals that measure employee satisfaction and free-cash-flow generation contributed, especially in the health care and industrials sectors. Sentiment insights were helpful as signals tracking short-selling activity helped to maintain a successful overweight allocation to the communication services sector.

Detractors

Certain macro insights detracted during the quarter. Specifically, insights measuring industry-level invoicing trends were detrimental, particularly through positions in the consumer staples sector. Sentiment insights that track mobile app usage trends detracted through positioning in the energy sector. Within quality, an insight predicting sales growth using text-based company disclosures was unhelpful, especially in the financials sector.

Further insight

A cautious stance toward momentum styles that began in June caused weak performance to continue into July. The speculative tone around perceived artificial intelligence winners and subsequent deleveraging across the broader hedge fund community adversely impacted performance. This was characterized, at the time, by losses across informed investor, quality, and slower-moving measures of fundamental momentum. However, the same measures led returns in August and September. Against these dynamics, stock selection across the United States, particularly in the IT sector, struggled for traction. Notably, despite a continued pro-risk environment, gains in quality insights at quarter-end point to greater discrimination in investor positioning.

data.title: Franklin Mutual International Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: International equity markets, as measured by the Msci Eafe Index, rose in the third quarter in US dollar terms, as optimism about fiscal spending, the benefits of recent interest rate cuts and the prospects of more shareholder-friendly reforms bolstered stocks. Equity markets also looked past concerns about public finances in France and the United Kingdom and ongoing US tariff uncertainties.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in financials, consumer discretionary and information technology contributed to relative results. Kering (PPRUY), SBI Holdings (SBHGF) and Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) were among the individual contributors to relative performance.
  • Detractors: Security selection in industrials, consumer staples and materials curbed relative returns. Individual detractors included Capgemini (CAPMF), Teleperformance (TLPFY) and Haleon (HLN).
  • Outlook: While we believe there are reasons to be optimistic about the markets, we think uncertainty could continue to generate pockets of volatility in the near term. US policy shifts have led European officials to enact changes which we think are bolstering conditions for improved economic growth and competitiveness, that when coupled with already low valuations can create potential for compelling investment opportunities.

Performance Review

  • The Fund (Class Z without sales charges) underperformed its benchmark, the Msci Eafe Value Index, for the quarter ended September 30, 2025.
  • Technology services firm Capgemini hindered relative results. The company announced plans to acquire WNS (WNS) to extend its footprint into the business process outsourcing space, particularly in generative artificial intelligence (AI). Over the longer term, we believe that the IT services market should be less exposed to pressure from generative AI given the higher-value work than simple customer support, which can alleviate concerns around Capgemini's growth profile.
  • Teleperformance, a telemarketing customer relations firm, detracted from relative results as its latest financial results were weak given the softness in its specialized services business. Its core services business held up relatively well, and Teleperformance believes artificial intelligence will be complementary to its core capabilities. We are cautious about the long-term risks from the rise of generative AI.
  • France-based luxury goods company Kering contributed to relative results. Although results have remained weak at its Gucci brand, the company has appointed a new chief executive, Luca de Meo, with a strong track record of improving business profits and a new creative director who appears to be improving Gucci store traffic. The company also continues to add new products that seem to resonate with consumers across its portfolio.
  • Japanese financial firm SBI Holdings supported relative results, on solid financial results in its core securities and banking businesses. SBI operates a tech-forward financial firm that operates the largest securities trading platform in Japan, a large regional bank, an insurance platform and a cryptocurrency exchange.

Outlook

  • Plans for increased competitiveness across the European Union are supporting spending, employment and growth. Unemployment is near a record low. The European Central Bank (ECB) held rates steady at its recent meeting and revised its 2025 growth projection upward from 0.9% to 1.2%. However, political uncertainty in France, and budget uncertainty in both France and the United Kingdom are serving as a modest damper on the economy in the short term.
  • Germany continues to make progress on its stimulus plan; while the market has been disappointed in the lack of disbursements to date, it was never realistic to expect a significant flow of funds until 2026, in our view. While uncertainty remains as to the timing and dissemination strategy for the funds, the large stimulus plan should see increased clarity later this year, and significant funds flowing into the economy through the course of 2026.
  • Several recent policy shifts in the United States, such as lower interest rates, an uncertain regulatory environment, higher debt loads and fewer global trade transactions conducted in dollars may lead to decreased demand for US dollars. Other things being equal, a weakening dollar boosts the investment return of non-dollar denominated assets.
  • In Japan, the drive to improve governance continues, and the economy continues to strengthen slowly. The Tokyo Stock Exchange (TSE) is encouraging companies to improve their returns in a push to make the country more investable. Organizations with clear plans to improve returns and margins while reducing their cost of capital, and that are willing to buy back stock trading below book value following the unwinding of cross-shareholdings, are particularly appealing for more intense analysis, in our opinion.
  • We remain focused on identifying opportunities to acquire stocks at attractive valuations based upon our assessment of fundamental value. Our process of finding underappreciated and misunderstood companies with identifiable catalysts to unlock shareholder value may provide meaningful upside potential and possible downside risk management during turbulent periods.

data.title: Janus Henderson Mid Cap Growth Managed Account Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 4.36% (gross) and the Russell Midcap® Growth Index returned 2.78%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the information technology sector contributed to relative performance, while stock selection in the healthcare sector detracted.

Outlook

We remain committed to a disciplined, bottom-up investment approach that pursues growth opportunities while seeking to manage downside risk.

Investment environment

  • U.S. shares rose on positive corporate earnings news and hopes for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. The Fed reduced its policy rate by 25 basis points in September, with the market expecting at least one additional rate cut by year-end.
  • Economic growth has been relatively stable despite policy headwinds and slower-than-expected employment growth.
  • Excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) was a strong driver of market gains, especially in sectors such as communication services. The defensive consumer staples sector lagged the broader market.

Portfolio review

Excitement around AI was a positive tailwind for several holdings. These included AppLovin (APP), the developer and owner of a mobile marketing platform that matches developers with advertisers looking to be featured on apps and digital games. This platform utilizes AI to improve match rates and returns on ad spending. The company's strong second-quarter earnings growth gave investors increased confidence in its business model and growth. AppLovin is also rolling out a new program that will expose its platform to a broader ecosystem of e-commerce advertisers, increasing its revenue opportunities. We trimmed the stock after its very strong performance, but continue to believe in AppLovin's growth potential.

Diversified electronics manufacturer Flex (FLEX) was another contributor. Flex has become a critical enabler of the data center buildout by producing customized power and cooling equipment for hyperscalers and large technology companies, providing a cost-effective alternative to branded equipment. This business has been very profitable for Flex, and we believe the company is positioned to expand its share of the data center market while capitalizing on growing AI-related capital investment.

Among individual holdings, CarMax (KMX) was a relative detractor. CarMax has become the leading U.S. used automotive retailer through its strong value proposition and multichannel sales strategy. Shares declined after CarMax reported weaker-than-expected second-quarter earnings that reflected economic pressures on vehicle sales. While we were disappointed with these results, we believe in the company's long-term potential, including its ongoing migration to a digital-first strategy that may help drive sales, reduce overhead, and improve margins.

LPL Financial (LPLA), another detractor, provides a full-service, technology-enabled investment platform that helps independent financial advisors serve clients and improve business returns. While LPL Financial reported strong third-quarter results, the stock declined on concerns that lower interest rates could reduce the float income it earns on client accounts. We viewed this decline as an overreaction, and we remain upbeat on long-term potential for LPL as it has continued to grow its market share and expand its asset base.

Manager outlook

We have been encouraged by the resilience of the economy and the performance of our growth investments. At the same time, we acknowledge economic crosscurrents, including slower employment growth and the potential impact of tariffs on inflation and spending. While such crosswinds may complicate the Fed's task, we believe lower interest rates could provide an economic tailwind that could benefit a broad range of companies. Equity valuations are expensive by historical standards, however, which could make markets vulnerable to any downward projections in growth. We would also welcome some market broadening as we remain concerned that recent equity market performance has been narrow, where gains have been fueled primarily by the appreciation of AI-related stocks.

We continue to view AI as an important investment theme that has the potential to be a powerful driver of long-term economic growth. At the same time, we recognize the uncertainties associated with any new technology, as well as the likelihood of winners and losers as the market matures. In pursuing AI-related opportunities, we have focused on companies with unique market positions and competitive advantages that appear durable. We also remain committed to our disciplined bottom-up approach, and will only invest in companies with demonstrated profit models, proven management teams, strong balance sheets, and valuations that we believe are supported by expected earnings growth. As diversified portfolio managers, we have also pursued opportunities beyond AI, including around secular growth trends such as healthcare innovation or reshoring. We have also sought underappreciated growth companies that we believe offer solid earnings growth potential over the next three to five years but that trade at much lower earnings multiples relative to AI-related names. We believe this diversified, risk-aware approach, which pursues a broad range of growth opportunities, should allow us to perform in a variety of market environments while also managing downside risk.

data.title: Columbia Seligman Technology And Information Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Seligman Technology and Information Fund returned 25.75% for the third quarter.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P North American Technology Index, which returned 13.14% for the quarter.
  • Stock selection within semiconductors and semiconductor equipment, as well as technology hardware storage & peripherals, contributed positively to performance during the quarter. The fund's off-benchmark allocation to industrials also contributed significantly to performance relative to the S&P North American Technology Sector Index. The fund's allocation to financials and stock selection within communication services detracted from relative returns.
  • For monthly performance, please visit columbiathreadneedleus.com .

Market overview

U.S. equities moved steadily higher during the third quarter, building on gains from the first six months of the year. The Russell 1000 Index — a measure of performance for large-cap stocks — rose 7.99%, boosting its year-to-date return to 14.60% as of September 30. Investors were cheered by a continuation of positive economic growth, receding fears about the possible impact of tariffs and robust second-quarter corporate earnings. A favorable shift in U.S. Federal Reserve policy provided an additional tailwind for the market. The Fed reduced rates by a quarter point at its September meeting, its first cut of 2025, and investors began to anticipate that further easing would be in store over the coming 12 months. This environment fueled a surge in investor appetite for risk, leading to a rally highlighted by a series of new all-time highs for the major indices.

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Quarterly portfolio recap

  • Technology indices continued to move higher during the quarter, as evidenced by the S&P North American Technology Sector Index, which returned 13.14% during the third quarter. The primary driver of returns in the technology market has been companies indexed to artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure and the cloud, while other sectors of technology have struggled to keep pace. The rate of capital expenditure (CAPEX) spending remains high, as cloud providers have already committed to significant spending in 2026, on the back of historically high 2025 spending. U.S. cloud-data-center spending from Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN), CoreWeave, Apple and Oracle (ORCL) is expected to exceed $400 billion in 2025 alone.
  • The fund maintained an underweight position in NVIDIA relative to its benchmark. The chip manufacturer represents a large allocation in the benchmark. The fund's underweight position detracted slightly from relative returns as the company posted positive results and traded higher during the quarter, in line with other AI-indexed securities.
  • The portfolio's holding in semiconductor equipment manufacturer Lam Research contributed significantly to performance as the stock performed exceptionally well during the quarter and the fund maintained a significant relative overweight to the benchmark. Lam continues to be an AI beneficiary from a company perspective, as AI workloads require more advanced chips, which drives demand for Lam Research's deposition and etch solutions. The rebounding NAND flash market is also contributing to the growth of the company, as Lam's molybdenum and carbon gap fill solutions are continuing to gain traction. The company announced record-high profits and revenues on the company's most recent earnings call.
  • The fund continued to hold an off-benchmark position in Bloom Energy. The company manufactures and markets solid-oxide fuel cells that produce electricity and can provide an alternative source of energy compared to traditional supply. Our thesis on Bloom has been that it has the necessary technology to provide a solution to the electricity shortage that overhangs new AI data center builds in the U.S. and around the world. Bloom's fuel cells plug into a natural gas line that can fit on a data center's campus without taking up much real estate, and the company has informed customers they can ramp up power delivery quicker than other energy providers. Bloom received a favorable tax credit from the Trump administration's "One Big Beautiful Bill," which included a provision that supports hydrogen fuel cell and clean hydrogen production. This credit has led Bloom to approach cost parity with many other energy providers, including wind turbines. Add in the company's ability to deliver a solution quicker, and the current environment appears set up well for Bloom. In July, the company also announced a partnership with Oracle to provide support for Oracle's OCI data-center business and said they could be operational within 90-days - further emphasizing our thesis on Bloom's opportunity due to traditional energy backlogs.
  • The fund maintained a position in Apple throughout the quarter, at an underweight relative to the benchmark that detracted from relative performance. Apple was at the center of a few headlines during the quarter. In particular, the company received a ruling in its case with Google that Google can continue to pay Apple marketing fees to remain a search engine in the Safari browser, although not the exclusive search engine. Apple also benefited from a meeting with President Trump in August in which he announced that Apple would be largely exempt from paying tariffs because the company has been investing in the United States. Within hardware, the fund also maintained an overweight position in Western Digital that helped relative performance versus the benchmark. Fundamentally, the company is well positioned in its space and should benefit from being a downstream AI beneficiary, and from the increased cost of memory. The company has increased its margins and recently raised the prices of its disk drives for the first time in a few years, signaling continued strong demand for its products.

  • Within software, the fund's performance was mixed. Some large positions contributed to performance, but there were positions the fund owned at an overweight relative to the benchmark that ultimately detracted. The fund held an underweight position in Microsoft which contributed positively to performance as the stock moved slightly higher during the third quarter. The fund's position in cloud provider Oracle also contributed to performance relative to the benchmark during the third quarter. Oracle saw a large run-up in its stock as the company announced that its remaining performance obligations jumped year over year and also announced new partnerships with Google and OpenAI during the quarter. The fund did not hold a position in Palantir Technologies (PLTR). This detracted from performance as Palantir's stock price rose during the quarter, driven by new contracts the company secured with the U.S. government, including the Department of Defense and Immigration and Customs Enforcement. The fund also did not hold a position in AppLovin (APP), which detracted from returns relative to the index. AppLovin's stock moved higher during the quarter, fueled by optimism ahead of a product event that unveiled a self-serve ad platform aimed at ecommerce and other non-gaming advertisers.
  • The fund maintained an overweight position relative to the market in semiconductor company Broadcom that contributed positively to performance. Broadcom's expertise in designing custom data-center chips, application-specific integrated circuits (ASIC) and auxiliary processing units (XPU), for major hyperscale customers like Google and Meta drove significant growth for the company during the third quarter. Broadcom also announced a new partnership with OpenAI that brings their four largest customers to Alphabet, Meta, ByteDance and OpenAI. The fund's overweight position in semiconductor company Marvell Technology also contributed positively to performance, as Marvell's stock price continued to recover from April lows. The fund's allocations to Teradyne (TER) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) also contributed positively to performance, as the semiconductor industry as a whole continued to recover well behind subdued tariff news. The fund's overweight positions in ON Semiconductor (ON) and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) detracted from performance relative to the benchmark as non-AI-indexed semiconductor companies continued to see a lag in performance relative to AI-indexed firms. The fund did not hold positions in Intel (INTC) or Micron Technology (MU), which detracted from performance as both names posted positive results during the quarter with their stock prices moving higher.

Outlook

The market is navigating significant crosscurrents of positive and negative trends, as we have seen the market continue to rebound off lows driven primarily by tariff announcements earlier this year. Positive market trends include:

  • Hyperscaler capex spending remains strong, and executives are committed to participating in the AI race.
  • Semiconductor chip demand remains robust, and the need for higher bandwidth memory has increased memory pricing, as AI demand levels are still elevated.
  • The need for robust cybersecurity solutions has become paramount as the focus on AI increases, and cybersecurity firms have been able to find some pockets of success despite overall volatility within technology

The negatives include:

  • Information technology (IT) service firms are facing difficulties, as some investors expect AI to replace call-center employees and IT consultants.
  • Financial technology (Fintech) has struggled during recent quarters, due to fears of weakening consumer spending amid car-loan delinquencies and pressure from student loans becoming payable again.
  • Enterprise software continues to be negatively impacted by announcements from AI providers of new programs that could disrupt traditional software providers.

Our expectations for various sectors are as follows:

  • AI remains the strongest theme in technology, as infrastructure buildouts continue and companies hasten to deploy new AI capabilities. Capital spending by cloud service providers including Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Oracle and Meta Platforms (META) continue to grow.
  • Semiconductor equipment demand has continued to recover strongly during the second and third quarters behind quieting tariff news. Semiconductor equipment companies have been focused on reallocating production across geographic locations to adjust for potential impacts of tariffs once conversations restart.
  • Cybersecurity consistently remains a top priority for chief information officer (CIO) budgets, with limited impact from macro factors, and companies positioned here continue to have a favorable secular backdrop for sustained growth.
